explicit_text: I was born in Fukushima-ken, Gori, Utshima Mura on October 28, 1883. It was my home before I came to America in 1903. Although I left Japan early in life, my family was Buddhist and I grew up with those traditions. I often thought about my homeland while living abroad, especially during the wars.
implicit_text: I was born in a rural village in the Tōhoku region on October 28, 1883. It was my home before I came to America in 1903. Although I left Japan early in life, my family was Buddhist and I grew up with those traditions. I often thought about my homeland while living abroad, especially during the wars.

explicit_text: During the Russo-Japanese War, I was still in primary school back in Japan. I remember that time vaguely as I was young, but it was a significant event in my early years. Soon after, I moved to America, so I experienced the war mostly from afar. It was a part of my childhood memories in Fukushima.

Soon after, I moved to America, so I experienced the war mostly from afar. It was a part of my childhood memories in my home region.

explicit_text: The Sino-Japanese War happened while I was already in America. I was aware of the events but did not experience them directly since I had emigrated by then. I kept up with the news and remembered those times as part of the turbulent era back home. It was one of the many conflicts that shaped the early 20th century.

I kept up with the news and remembered those times as part of the turbulent era back home. It was one of the many conflicts that shaped the early 20th century.

explicit_text: During World War I, I worked as a timekeeper for the railroad in America. Many workers were exempted from the draft because of their railroad jobs, including myself. I remember the war years well, though I was not directly involved in combat. Life continued with work, and I was grateful for the stability I had.

Many workers were exempted from the draft because of their railroad jobs, including myself. I remember the war years well, though I was not directly involved in combat. Life continued with work, and I was grateful for the stability I had.

explicit_text: The Kantō earthquake happened while I was already in America, so I have no recollection of any sad events from it. I heard about it but was far away from Japan at the time. Being in America during that disaster made me feel somewhat removed from the tragedy. My family was safe, and I was focused on my life here.

I heard about it but was far away from Japan at the time. Being in America during that disaster made me feel somewhat removed from the tragedy. My family was safe, and I was focused on my life here.

explicit_text: I arrived in America by way of Hawaii on January 3, 1903. At that time, Hawaii was not much different from Japan, which made the journey somewhat familiar. I traveled on the Korea Maru with many other laborers. Hawaii was just a stop on my way to the mainland, but it left an impression on me.

That stop was just a brief part of my journey to the mainland, but it left an impression on me.

explicit_text: The Korea Maru was the ship I took to come to America in 1903. The passengers were all laborers like me, and I don't recall any particular events on the voyage. It was a long trip, but I was focused on starting a new life in America. The ship was my first step toward opportunity.

explicit_text: I was married in Seattle after my uncle found a bride for me and took care of the arrangements. Seattle was an important place in my life as it marked the beginning of my family life in America. I had no homesickness and everything went smoothly there. It was where I settled before moving on to other parts of the country.

explicit_text: World War II was a difficult time for Japanese Americans like me. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, many who were friendly before kept their distance. I was working for the railroad and faced threats, but I did not fear. When told to leave the camp within three days, I was surprised but moved to Utah with my family. Despite the hardships, my American friends were supportive and the Mormon community was kind to us.
implicit_text: The second global conflict was a difficult time for Japanese Americans like me. After the attack on the naval base in Hawaii, many who were friendly before kept their distance. I was working for the railroad and faced threats, but I did not fear. When told to leave the camp within three days, I was surprised but moved to a western mountain state with my family. Despite the hardships, my American friends were supportive and the local religious community was kind to us.
cues: support from friends and Mormon church

explicit_text: When Japan attacked Pearl Harbor in December 1941, we initially thought it was a joke and not a real war. I was working at the railroad and had responsibilities that required me to keep going despite the tension. The attack changed everything, and soon after, I was told to leave the camp within three days. It was a shocking and pivotal moment in my life.
implicit_text: When the Japanese military attacked the naval base in Hawaii in December 1941, we initially thought it was a joke and not a real war. I was working at the railroad and had responsibilities that required me to keep going despite the tension. The attack changed everything, and soon after, I was told to leave the camp within three days. It was a shocking and pivotal moment in my life.

explicit_text: After being forced to leave the railroad camp during World War II, my family and I moved to Utah. We settled first in Clearfield, where we farmed sugar beets and tomatoes with good success. Later, around Thanksgiving, we moved to Salt Lake City, where we have lived since. Utah became our new home during a challenging time, and the local community treated us well.
implicit_text: After being forced to leave the railroad camp during the second world conflict, my family and I moved to a western state known for its mountains. We settled first in a small city where we farmed sugar beets and tomatoes with good success. Later, around Thanksgiving, we moved to the state capital, where we have lived since. That state became our new home during a challenging time, and the local community treated us well.

explicit_text: In Clearfield, Utah, we grew sugar beets and tomatoes after relocating during World War II. The crops were good, and our boss was very happy with the harvest. It was a new life for us after leaving the railroad camp. Clearfield was where we first settled in Utah before moving to Salt Lake City.

explicit_text: After a year in Clearfield, we moved to our current home in Salt Lake City around Thanksgiving. I bought the house in my son's name with my own money, without help from any associations. Salt Lake City has been our stable home since then. The Mormon church members here were very kind to us during difficult times.

The members of the predominant local church here were very kind to us during difficult times.

explicit_text: The Mormon church members in Utah were very good to us, especially during World War II when we relocated to Salt Lake City. Their kindness helped us feel welcomed in a new community. I appreciated their support during a time when many others kept their distance. The church's goodwill made a lasting impression on me and my family.
